paper_title: Quantitative data on plant macrofossil distribution in the holocene sediment cores of mires in the Kaliningrad region, Russian Federation (South-Eastern Baltic)

methods.description: Process for collecting peat core samples from the field using a specific corer, following standard methodological guidelines.

steps.description: The peat bed coring and peat samples retrieval were carried out by means of the Russian D-corer (model TBG-1) taking into account standard methodological guidelines [1].
steps.searchable_summary: Collect peat core samples using a Russian D-corer following standard guidelines.

methods.method_name: Peat Sample Preparation for Macrofossil Analysis
methods.description: Laboratory procedure for treating raw peat samples to isolate plant macro-remnants for microscopic analysis, including washing, sieving, and slide preparation.

steps.description: The laboratory treatment of sediment samples and specimen preparation were executed according to the standard technique [2,3].
steps.searchable_summary: Process raw peat samples according to standard laboratory techniques.

steps.description: in order to remove non-structured humus particles, a sample of wet peat was cleaned under running water
steps.searchable_summary: Wash wet peat samples under running water to remove humus.

steps.description: the washed material was retained on a 250 mm sieve in case of slightly decomposed peat
steps.searchable_summary: Sieve washed peat material using a 250 mm sieve for slightly decomposed peat.
outputs: Sieved peat material (retained on 250 mm sieve)

steps.description: and on a 100 mm sieve at a high degree of decomposition.
steps.searchable_summary: Sieve washed peat material using a 100 mm sieve for highly decomposed peat.
outputs: Sieved peat material (retained on 100 mm sieve)

steps.description: For the microscopic analysis, a specimen was prepared in the following way: a small amount of the washed peat has been placed on a microscope slide
steps.searchable_summary: Prepare microscope slides from washed peat material.

steps.description: several drops of water were added in order to spread the plant residues equally over the slide with a thin layer.
steps.searchable_summary: Add water to spread peat material evenly on the microscope slide.
outputs: Prepared microscope slide with evenly spread peat material

methods.description: Procedure for identifying and quantifying plant macro-remnants in prepared peat samples using microscopy, including identification aids and quality control.

steps.description: The specimen was studied under the microscope which included both identifications of plant macro-remnants and estimation of percentage for different systematic groups of plants in a peat sample.
steps.searchable_summary: Perform microscopic analysis to identify and quantify plant macro-remnants.

steps.description: No less than 4 standard microscope slides were examined for each peat sample.
steps.searchable_summary: Examine a minimum of 4 slides per sample for consistency and coverage.
inputs: Multiple prepared slides for one peat sample

steps.description: A number of atlases and taxonomic keys were used for identification of a systematic group of the plant residues [2,4e7].
steps.searchable_summary: Use atlases and taxonomic keys to aid plant residue identification.

steps.description: In some cases, plant residues were stained with methylene blue for identification purposes, in particular, for more distinct detection of pore shape and pore arrangement throughout a leaf.
steps.searchable_summary: Stain plant residues with methylene blue to enhance identification features.

methods.description: Procedure for determining the age of peat and gyttja samples using conventional radiocarbon dating and calibrating the results to calendar years.

steps.description: The radiocarbon analysis of peat and gyttja samples was performed by conventional (14C) dating at the Radiocarbon Laboratory of the Institute of Geography of Russian Academy of Sciences (Moscow, Russia).
steps.searchable_summary: Perform conventional radiocarbon (14C) dating on peat and gyttja samples.

steps.description: All radiocarbon dates were calibrated using the 14C calibration program CALIB, version 7.1.0 14ChronoCentre, QueensUniversityBelfast and the calibration curve IntCal13 [8].
steps.searchable_summary: Calibrate raw radiocarbon dates using CALIB software and IntCal13 curve.
